items.subtopic: Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.title: Mechanism of Action and Clinical Impact of BoNTs
items.content: Botulinum neurotoxins (BoNTs) target neurons by cleaving SNARE-family proteins, blocking neurotransmission and leading to flaccid paralysis. The irreversible binding results in prolonged paralysis until nerve regeneration.
keyPoints: BoNTs are metalloproteases composed of a light chain with catalytic activity and a heavy chain with translocation and receptor-binding domains.
Serotypes A and E cleave SNAP-25; B, D, F, G cleave VAMP; C cleaves SNAP-25 and syntaxin.
Cleavage disrupts synaptic fusion complexes, inhibiting acetylcholine release.
Paralysis persists for weeks or months until nerve regeneration.
items.reference: Botulism · Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.title: Toxicity and Dose-Response of BoNTs
items.content: BoNTs are extremely toxic, with lethal doses varying by route of exposure. The estimated human lethal dose via IV or IM is 0.1–1 ng/kg; inhalation ranges from 1–75 ng/kg.
keyPoints: BoNTs are among the most toxic substances known.
Lethal dose (IV/IM): 0.1–1 ng/kg of body weight.
Oral route toxicity is lower: 0.1–1 μg/kg.
items.reference: Botulism · Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.title: Genetic Diversity and Classification of BoNTs
items.content: BoNTs are encoded by the bont gene with at least 40 unique subtypes within serotypes A, B, E, and F. Variants differ by at least 2.6% in amino acid sequence.
keyPoints: Seven distinct BoNT serotypes: A through G.
Serotypes A, B, E, F cause disease in humans.
At least 40 unique subtypes within serotypes A, B, E, and F.
Variant designation requires at least a 2.6% difference in amino acid sequence.
items.reference: Botulism · Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Understanding the Different Forms of Botulism
items.content: Botulism can manifest in various forms, each with distinct etiologies and pathogeneses. These include foodborne botulism from contaminated foods, wound botulism from infected wounds, infant botulism due to intestinal colonization in infants ≤1 year, adult intestinal colonization affecting those >1 year, iatrogenic botulism from therapeutic BoNT injections, and potential aerosolized use as a bioweapon.
keyPoints: Four naturally occurring forms of botulism: foodborne, wound, infant, adult intestinal colonization.
Two non-naturally occurring forms: iatrogenic botulism and aerosolized use as a bioweapon.
Foodborne botulism is the most common form reported in many countries.

items.title: Public Health Implications of Foodborne Botulism
items.content: Foodborne botulism, caused by ingestion of preformed BoNT in contaminated foods, is a significant public health concern due to its potential for causing outbreaks. Each case represents an emergency that requires immediate attention and intervention.
keyPoints: Foodborne botulism involves ingestion of food containing preformed toxin.
It is the most common form reported in many countries.
Each case is a public health emergency with outbreak potential.

items.subtopic: Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.question: Which Clostridium species are known to produce botulinum neurotoxins?
options: Clostridium butyricum and Clostridium baratii only
Clostridium botulinum, Clostridium butyricum, and Clostridium baratii
items.explanation: Botulinum neurotoxins are produced by Clostridium botulinum, Clostridium butyricum, and Clostridium baratii.
items.reference: Botulism · Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.question: Which SNARE-family protein is cleaved by BoNT serotypes A and E?
items.explanation: Serotypes A and E of BoNT cleave SNAP-25.
items.reference: Botulism · Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.question: What is the estimated human lethal dose range for BoNT acquired via inhalation?
items.explanation: The estimated human lethal dose of BoNT acquired by inhalation is 1–75 ng/kg.
items.reference: Botulism · Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.question: What role do hemagglutinin proteins encoded by *ha* genes play in the pathogenesis of botulism?
options: They facilitate toxin absorption across epithelial barriers.
They increase thermal resistance of spores.
items.explanation: Hemagglutinin proteins encoded by *ha* genes facilitate the absorption of toxins across the epithelial barrier.
items.reference: Botulism · Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.question: Which form of botulism occurs when a patient given injections of BoNT experiences signs of systemic botulism?
items.explanation: Iatrogenic botulism occurs when a patient given injections of BoNT experiences signs of systemic botulism.

items.question: What is the primary route of entry for foodborne botulism?
items.explanation: Foodborne botulism occurs through ingestion of foods containing preformed toxin.

items.question: Which form of botulism involves the colonization of Clostridium species in the intestinal tract of infants?
items.explanation: Infant botulism is caused by BoNT-producing species of Clostridium colonizing the intestinal tract of infants.

items.subtopic: Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.statement: Botulinum neurotoxins block neurotransmission by cleaving SNARE-family proteins.
items.explanation: BoNTs inhibit acetylcholine release by cleaving specific SNARE-family proteins, blocking neurotransmission.
items.reference: Botulism · Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.statement: The lethal dose of BoNT via the oral route is higher than that for inhalation.
items.explanation: The estimated human lethal dose of BoNT acquired by the oral route is much lower than that for inhalation.
items.reference: Botulism · Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.statement: Wound botulism occurs when spores of BoNT-producing species of Clostridium contaminate a wound and produce toxin.
items.explanation: This statement accurately describes the pathogenesis of wound botulism.

items.statement: Adult intestinal colonization by BoNT-producing species is similar to infant botulism and affects persons ≤1 year of age.
items.explanation: Adult intestinal colonization affects persons >1 year of age, unlike infant botulism.

items.subtopic: Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.assertion: BoNTs are among the most toxic substances known.
items.reason: Extremely small amounts of BoNT can cause severe disease and death.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, and the reason correctly explains why BoNTs are considered extremely toxic.
items.reference: Botulism · Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.assertion: C. botulinum spores can be destroyed by boiling.
items.reason: Boiling ensures the destruction of C. botulinum spores under most environmental conditions.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, and the reason correctly explains that boiling destroys C. botulinum spores.
items.reference: Botulism · Carolina Lúquez, Jeremy Sobel | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
